coredata.dc:title: Rapid detection of tryptamine by optosensor with molecularly imprinted polymers based on carbon dots-embedded covalent-organic frameworks
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Dianwei
Wang, Yanping
Geng, Weitao
Liu, Huilin
coredata.dc:description: A rapid optosensing detection method for tryptamine (TRY) based on one-pot surface-imprinting synthesis methods has been developed. The optosensor consists of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) based on carbon dots (CNs)-embedded covalent-organic frameworks (COFs). The principle of operation depends on adsorbents providing a large number of accessible recognition sites with high affinity and specificity, to achieve both a rapid uptake of and high capacity for TRY. After removing the template molecules, the desired molecular cavities were selected and fixed in a distinct manner to provide a defined chemical microenvironment for the reversible binding of the TRY target molecules. The optosensing system relied on the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the TRY-imprinted polymers and the concentration of TRY in the range of 0.025–0.4 mg kg−1with a detection limit of 7 μg kg−1. The system was used for efficiently detecting TRY in meat samples with recovery values from 91.42% to 119.80%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tryptamine
Carbon nanodots
Covalent organic frameworks
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Meat

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs

The static analysis of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
Interference analysis of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
The reproducibility and stability of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
The mechanism of molecular recognition for MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
Characterization of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
Kinetic adsorption of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
TYR detection by MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
Specificity of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
The reproducibility and stability of the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs, (b) SEM image of NIPs based on CN-embedded COFs, (c) FT-IR spectra of MIPs and NIPs based on CN-embedded COFs, (d) XRD spectrum of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs, (e) XRD spectrum of NIPs based on CN-embedded COFs, (f) TGA of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs.
The kinetic binding of TRY onto MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs at a TRY concentration of 0.4 mg kg−1.

(a) Stern–Volmer plot of TRY concentration and the fluorescence intensity of TRY-MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs, (b) Fluorescence spectra of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs in the presence of increasing TYR concentrations from 0.025 to 0.4 mg kg−1.
The specificity determined using the Stern–Volmer plot for MIPs and NIPs based on CN-embedded COFs for TRY, HIS and TYR.
The stability of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs from 5 to 30 days at room temperature.
A scheme for synthesis of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs.
TYR detection in meat samples by optosensor.

row.entry:
Pork Sausage 2
Chicken Sausage 1
Chicken Sausage 2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spiked recovery results for the determination of TRY in pork sausage 1.

head.ce:title: Rapid detection of tryptamine by optosensor with molecularly imprinted polymers based on carbon dots-embedded covalent-organic frameworks

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science & Technology, 29 The Thirteenth Road, Tianjin Economy and Technology Development Area, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
Tianjin Economy and Technology Development Area

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, Beijing Technology and Business University, 11 Fucheng Road, Beijing, 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A rapid optosensing detection method for tryptamine (TRY) based on one-pot surface-imprinting synthesis methods has been developed. The optosensor consists of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) based on carbon dots (CNs)-embedded covalent-organic frameworks (COFs). The principle of operation depends on adsorbents providing a large number of accessible recognition sites with high affinity and specificity, to achieve both a rapid uptake of and high capacity for TRY. After removing the template molecules, the desired molecular cavities were selected and fixed in a distinct manner to provide a defined chemical microenvironment for the reversible binding of the TRY target molecules. The optosensing system relied on the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the TRY-imprinted polymers and the concentration of TRY in the range of 0.025–0.4 mg kg−1with a detection limit of 7 μg kg−1. The system was used for efficiently detecting TRY in meat samples with recovery values from 91.42% to 119.80%.

ce:section.ce:para: Tryptamine (TRY), a biogenic amine, is a nitrogenous organic compound of low molecular weight [1], derived from the enzymatic decarboxylation of free tryptophan and proteins [2]. It is present in a wide variety of foods, especially protein-rich foods such as meat and dairy products, beverages and other fermented foods [3]. TRY levels usually increase during fermentation and maturation with lactic acid bacteria. The presence of TRY in food processing has been associated with the properties of the raw materials, the conditions of production, and a lack of hygiene during food manufacturing. A high concentration of TRY accumulating in the human body may cause symptoms such as nausea, respiratory discomfort, hot flushes, cold sweats, palpitations, headaches, red rashes, and high or low blood pressure [4]. However, determining the levels of TRY is not simple because of its structure and the low levels occurring in complex food samples. Until recently, TRY has been determined by chromatographic techniques such as capillary chromatography [5], high performance liquid chromatography [6], high performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [7], and ion chromatography [8]. Chromatographic determination, often involving tedious separation, is time-consuming and relatively expensive. Skerritt et al. used convenient immunoassays to detect TRY and tyramine [9] where selectivity was the major criterion for its successful determination and application.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have become very attractive biomimetic recognition phases for the selective capture of small organic molecules or groups of structurally related species [10]. MIPs can be produced much more cost-effectively and are distinctly more stable than biological analogs such as antibodies [11]. Molecular imprinting results in a three-dimensional polymer network wrapped around analyte molecules, and hollow structures complementary in their size, shape, and orientation of recognition sites to the analyte molecules. The template molecules are imprinted in the polymeric matrix. An important step in the creation of MIPs is removing the template molecules during the polymerization process, which generates the molecular cavities [12]. The presence of template residues in the MIPs is a serious drawback when using MIP if they are applied as stationary phases in liquid chromatography columns or in solid phase extraction cartridges. However, when MIP is applied as a recognition unit in a sensing system to avoid these issues, the residue does not interfere very much because the analytical signal is generated only when a molecule binds to its imprinted cavity. Xing et al. have used electrochemical sensors based on electrodes modified with molecularly imprinted films on polypyrrole-sulfonated graphene/hyaluronic acid-multiwalled carbon nanotubes to determine TRY. The proposed electrochemical sensor showed high selectivity, sensitivity and speed, appropriate for tryptamine detection, including in real sample analysis [3].

Carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene and carbon nanotubes, have attracted much research attention because of their electronic, optical, and biocompatible properties [13–16]. Carbon nanodots (CNs), one of the most promising fluorescent nanoparticles, exhibit unique optical properties, green synthesis, excellent solubility, easy functionalization, stable photoluminescence, high electrochemical activity, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [17–24]. CNs have been widely used in biosensors [14,25–27], bioimaging [28,29], optoelectronics [30], and photo catalysis [31]. To increase adsorption properties, covalent organic frameworks (COFs) can be introduced into a sensing system. COFs have a suitable combination of properties that make them potentially useful as excellent sorbents for small organic molecules [32,33]. Most COFs have a highly tunable molecular design, good crystallinity, and their eclipsed and ordered π-columnar structures enable their functional groups to be fully accessible and in close proximity with each other [34]. These advantages enhance the binding affinity and uptake capacity of the sensing system.

In the present study, we aimed to develop an efficient one-pot strategy to prepare optosensing material using MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescence efficiency should greatly improve through the spatial confinement in the CNs nanostructures, their enhanced radiative recombination rates, and their suppressed nonradiative recombination rates. Thus the MIPs are intended to selectively and sensitively sense the bonding interactions between COFs and the target molecules, and further transduce these chemical events to detectable fluorescence signals with blue luminescent CNs. Selective molecular cavities will be generated in the MIPs using methacrylic acid (MAA) and acrylamide (AM) as functional monomers. An ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) gate surface, coated with the TRY-templated MIPs, will rapidly and selectively respond to TRY analytes in the meat samples. One advantage of this approach is that the optosensor should be able to discriminate between a homologous series of TRY analytes that differ in their three-dimensional structure.

The chemicals used for synthesizing the CNs were N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMS, 97% purity, Tokyo Chemical Industry Co. Ltd., Tokyo, Japan), and anhydrous citric acid (99.5% purity, Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China). The COFs were synthesized using 1,3,5-triformylphloroglucinol (Tp; 99% purity, Strem Chemicals Inc., Newburyport, MA, USA), P-phenylenediamine (Pa), mesitylene (99% purity, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China), and dioxane (99.5% purity, J&K Scientific Ltd., Beijing, China). The other chemicals used for MIP synthesis were MAA (99% purity, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing Co., Ltd., Beijing, China), AM and EGDMA (98% purity, J&K Scientific Ltd.), and 2,2-azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN; 99% purity, Beijing Solarbio Science and Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). TRY and its structural and functional analogs histamine (HIS), and tyramine (TYR) were obtained from J&K Scientific Ltd. The other solvents and starting materials were used as received. The samples of pork and chicken sausage, pork and chicken ham, bacon, and smoked pork were collected from a local market.

A Hitachi S-4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) was used to observe the surface morphologies of the MIPs or non-imprinted polymers (NIPs) based on CN-embedded COFs. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (4000–400 cm−1) were recorded using KBr pellets in a FT-IR spectrophotometer (Vertex70vxrd, Bruker AXS GmbH, Karlsruhe, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a D/max-2500 X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Corp., Tokyo, Japan), with Cu K-alpha radiation. Thermo gravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a PTC-10 A thermal gravimetric analyzer (Rigaku Corp.) under N2 from room temperature to 900 °C at a ramp rate of 26 °C min−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
ce:section.ce:para: The CNs were synthesized via the one-step reaction reported by Wang et al. [21]. The specific synthesis steps were as follows. Ten mL of AEAPMS were degassed in a 100-mL three-necked flask with N2 for 5 min then heated to 240 °C. Anhydrous citric acid (0.5 g) was then added with vigorous stirring, and the mixture kept at 240 °C for 1 min. After cooling to room temperature, the CNs were purified by precipitation from petroleum ether three times, and then dispersed in a certain amount of ethanol for the subsequent experiments.

The TpPa COFs were synthesized using Schiff-base reactions] as follows [35]. A total of 0.3 mmol of Tp, 0.45 mmol of Pa, 1.5 mmol of acetic acid, and 3 mL of mesitylene/dioxane (1:1) were added to a 500-mL conical flask. The mixture was sonicated for 10 min, flushed with nitrogen to exclude air then heated at 120 °C for 3 days. The red precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed with anhydrous acetone. The powder was then solvent-exchanged with anhydrous acetone six times and dried at 180 °C under vacuum for 24 h.

The preparation of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs used one-pot surface-imprinting synthesis methods. The CNs (1 mL) were dissolved in methanol (3 mL), mixed with the COFs (1 mg), and then stirred for 3 h at room temperature in the dark. Then TRY (1 mmol) mixed with MAA (4 mmol), and AM (4 mmol) was added. The mixture was stirred for 30 min before adding AIBN (45 mg) and EGDMA (10 mmol) and then the mixture was heated at 60 °C in a water bath for 20 h. The TRY template molecules in the imprinted polymer were extracted with methanol via an H-bonding interaction. The cavities were specifically identified by extraction and drying in a vacuum oven at 50 °C for 4 h. At the same time, the NIPs were prepared using the same procedure but without the template molecules for comparison with the MIPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The adsorption equilibrium of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption equilibrium of the MIPs based on CNs-embedded COFs was evaluated by the fluorescence response in the presence of 0.4 mg kg−1 TRY at different times (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 45 and 55 min).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The static analysis of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
ce:section.ce:para: A series of TRY/ethanol solutions with concentrations of 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4 mg kg−1 were prepared to determine the relationship between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of the TRY template molecule. In the static study, 1 mg of the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs were dispersed in 200 μL TRY/ethanol solution in a Labsystems 96-well plate. The detection protocols for the NIPs and MIPS were the same as described above. The ratio of the Stern–Volmer constant (K SV) values for the MIPs and NIPs was defined as the imprinting factor for evaluating the selectivity of the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference analysis of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
ce:section.ce:para: The interference analysis used several kinds of biogenic amines, such as HIS and TYR for comparison with the TRY template molecule. For this study, 1 mg of the MIPs or NIPs based on CN-embedded COFs were dispersed in 200 μL ethanol solution then various concentrations (0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4 mg kg−1) of TRY, HIS and TYR standard solutions were added. The ratio of the K SV values of TRY, HIS and TYR were compared to evaluate the selection performance of the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The reproducibility and stability of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
ce:section.ce:para: Evaluating the reproducibility and stability of the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs was vital for the experimental process. The reproducibility of detection of TRY using MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs was investigated by measuring the fluorescent intensity at a TRY concentration of 0.4 mg kg−1. After each measurement, the MIPs were added again to a well, and the absorbed TRY molecules were eluted by methanol. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was determined by three repeated experiments to evaluate the reproducibility. The stability of the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs was evaluated by measuring the fluorescent intensity every 5 days over a period of 30 days at room temperature.

Ten grams of the minced meat samples were weighed directly into a centrifuge tube with 20 mL of ethanol solution then homogenized in a homogenizer for 2 min. The meat slurry samples obtained were centrifuged at 12,000 × g for 20 min at 4 °C. The ethanol layer was collected and the residue was re-extracted using the same procedure. The two ethanol extracts were combined and filtered to use for TRY analysis using a multi-functional enzyme-labeled instrument.

The fluorescence was measured using a multi-functional enzyme-labeled instrument (Biotek Instruments Inc., Winooski, VT, USA). The MIPs or NIPs based on CN-embedded COFs were added to a Labsystems 96-well plate then different concentrations of TRY or its analog standard solution were added. After thorough mixing, the plate was scanned using a Micro-plate Reader.

All the data were expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD), with each value being the mean of triplicate measurements. IBM SPSS Statistics 20.0 (IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA) was used to analyze the data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of molecular recognition for MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
ce:section.ce:para: The methods of Wang et al. were used to prepare stable fluorescent CNs using simple optimization conditions [21]. The basic procedure was as follows: the synthesis of blue fluorescent CNs including the decomposition and pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid was accompanied by the formation of gas and condensable vapors with the surface passivation reaction of the amine groups of AEAPMS with the carboxyl groups driven from the pyrolyzed species. The CNs were then added directly to the growth medium of the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs for incorporation into this one-pot synthesis process. Here, the COFs were prepared from the raw material, Tp and Pa, via Schiff-base reactions [35]. The preparation of TpPa COFs requires two steps: a reversible Schiff-base reaction that leads to the formation of a crystalline frame-work followed by an irreversible enol-to-keto tautomerization that enhances chemical stability. The desired selectivity of the MIPs was obtained through suitably designing their molecular cavities. These were fixed in a well-defined manner to provide a defined chemical microenvironment for reversibly binding the TRY target molecules. The type of functional monomer is very important for the formation of a stable polymerization complex. The functional monomers possessed recognition functionality that complemented the target molecule binding sites. MAA and AM were chosen as functional monomers for the TRY molecule. The carboxyl groups of MAA reacted with the amino groups of the TRY molecule through H–bonding interactions driving the template molecules into the surface of the matrix. Thus the recognition sites were imprinted with the template molecules in the composites. The fluorescent sensing platform was established through the strong TRY-MAA interaction, which can quench the fluorescence of CNs through charge transfer. AM reacted with the TpPa COFs via the Schiff-base reaction and also reacted with MAA by the covalent interaction with the carbon-carbon double bond. Because of the non-covalent interaction between the TRY and MAA molecules, methanol was used as the solution for removing the target molecule via a metathesis reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the successful synthesis of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs and to further investigate their physical and chemical properties, they were characterized using SEM, FT-IR, XRD and TGA (Fig. 1 ).

The SEM image clearly demonstrated that the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs were small and spherical on a scale of 10 μm (Fig. 1a). Their surface morphology was highly spherical with a rough surface and a relatively narrow size distribution centered on 500 nm at a scale of 1 μm (Fig. 1b). During the formation of the MIPs, some molecular cavities were left in the surface to specifically recognize the target molecules so their surface morphology was rough. Some reports have found that CNs are mono-disperse nanoparticles, and highly crystalline, with a spherical morphology. Analyzing the morphology of CNs by SEM was challenging because the average size of CNs was less than 10 nm. Using transmission electron microscopy to characterize the morphology of CNs was unsuccessful, because the AEAPMS-modified CNs tended to hydrolyze and self-condense to a gel, catalyzed by heat and/or trace water from the air during the drying process. The size distribution of the CNs was determined by dynamic light scattering analysis, and was about 3 nm in ethanol. In the present study, many CNs reacted with TpPa in the Schiff-base reaction to form a larger MIPs system. More CNs in the synthesized material enhanced its fluorescence properties, which in turn improved its optosensing by the sensors.

The MIPs and NIPs based on CN-embedded COFs were investigated using FT-IR analysis (Fig. 1c), their presence being confirmed by the appearance of several bands associated with the MIPs and NIPs at 1091.53, 1558.63, and 2932.86 cm−1. The FT-IR spectra of the MIPs and NIPs showed similar characteristic peaks. It is worth pointing out that their compositions were similar, with the template molecules being removed by extraction from the MIPs on the surface of the CNs during the imprinting process.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis was used to confirm the crystal structure of the synthesized MIPs (Fig. 1d) and NIPs (Fig. 1e) based on CN-embedded COFs. The XRD patterns of the MIPs and NIPs were similar with one broad reflection centered at about 4.3 Å, an indicator of disordered carbon, but in good agreement with the CNs pattern [21]. These results showed that the CNs had been embedded into the MIPs and NIPs.

TGA of the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs was also conducted in a flow of nitrogen (Fig. 1f). This was obviously only a two-stage decomposition from room temperature to 900 °C. The first-stage decomposition occurred at about 300 °C with a 10.57% weight loss due to the removal of the residual solvent and the decomposition of carboxyl groups in the MIPs. The second-stage decomposition occurred at about 800 °C, with a weight loss of 16.31% because of the decomposition of the framework. During the continuous heating, a weight loss of only about 26% illustrated the good thermal stability of the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic adsorption of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
ce:section.ce:para: A high concentration of the template molecule was used to evaluate the adsorption time by MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs. Fig. 2 shows that after adding 0.4 mg kg−1 TRY to the MIP sensing material, the fluorescence decreased immediately and exponentially over time. The large initial decrease in fluorescence intensity remained constant over time, indicating a high level of binding of the template to the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs. The response time of the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs was about 20 min. As the technique could detect 96 samples at the same time, such a fast sensing process allows a rapid detection in a real analysis system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TYR detection by MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
ce:section.ce:para:
For the dose-response experiments, the TRY concentration ranged from 0 to 0.5 mM and the fluorescence spectra of the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs were captured after an exposure time of 20 min. This allowed the relationship between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of the TRY molecule to be determined. The fluorescence quenching in this system followed the Stern–Volmer equation [36]:
F 0/F = 1 + K SV[TRY]
Where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher TRY, respectively, [TRY] is the concentration of the template molecule, and K sv is the Stern–Volmer constant.

The dependence of the F 0/F ratio on [TRY], is shown in Fig. 3 . with an R2 value of 0.9973. The linear range of the calibration curve was 0.025–0.4 mg kg−1. The detection limit, calculated following the 3σ IUPAC criterion, was 7.0 μg kg−1, a level comparable with other current detection techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of quenching-based TRY detection, an assay to illustrate TRY and MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs interactions was carried out (Fig. 4 ). This fluorescence quenching process was highly selective, indicating that detecting TRY in a highly complicated matrix was possible. Only weak fluorescence signals could be detected for the structural analogs HIS and TYR. The Ksv values of HIS- and TYR-MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs were 1.7697 and 0.8179, respectively, much lower values than that of TRY-MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs (4.6992). These results demonstrated that the present on-off fluorescence sensor was highly selective for TRY with an enhancement factor of 2.7- to 5.8-fold compared with the analogs tested. However, for the NIPs based on CN-embedded COFs, all the biogenic amines had very little effect, mainly because the binding sites on the NIP surface were limited because of its thinner shell. The imprinting factor, the ratio of K MIP to K NIP, was used to further evaluate the selectivity of the optosensing materials. Its value was 2.5, indicating that the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs could greatly enhance the quenching efficiency of fluorescence, thus enlarging the spectral sensitivity to the template molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The reproducibility and stability of the MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity changed slightly over 30 days providing favorable conditions for further detection (Fig. 5 ). Because of the protection offered by the TpPa framework and the MIP shell, the fluorescence intensity of the embedded CNs was much more stable. This clearly indicated that with time, the change in the fluorescence intensity of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs was negligible over 30 days suggesting that its stability was sufficient. These results agreed with those from TGA. The reproducibility of MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs was calculated using three independent experiments, with an average RSD of 2.3%. This showed that the prepared optosensing material had good reproducibility.

In conclusion, MIPs based on CN-embedded COFs were synthesized successfully using a one-pot surface-imprinting synthesis method, and were applied for the rapid optosensing determination of TRY in meat samples. The fluorescent sensing platform is based on the strong TRY-carboxyl interaction, which can quench the fluorescence of CNs through charge transfer. In the present study, TpPa COFs with well-defined mesopore structures displayed good affinity, stability and high capacity to serve as a scaffold for coordination sites to create ideal adsorbents. The optosensor has the advantages of reducing the TRY analysis time, and obtaining good recoveries and repeatability. This avoids the tedious and multi-step pre-treatment processing of food samples often considered a critical step in analysis and requiring other methods such as HPLC.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2018YFC1602300), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31822040), the Young Top-Notch Talent of High-Level Innovation and Entrepreneurs Support Program (2017000026833ZK28), the Innovative Research Team of Tianjin Municipral Education Commission (No. TD13-5015).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.01.092.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasound-assisted dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction combined with high-performance liquid chromatography-fluorescence detection for sensitive determination of biogenic amines in rice wine samples
Ion transfer voltammetry of tryptamine, serotonin, and tryptophan at the nitrobenzene/water interface
Electrochemical sensor based on molecularly imprinted film at polypyrrole-sulfonated graphene/hyaluronic acid-multiwalled carbon nanotubes modified electrode for determination of tryptamine
Reverse phase HPLC/DAD determination of biogenic amines as dansyl derivatives in experimental red wines
Simultaneous determination of food-related biogenic amines and precursor amino acids by micellar electrokinetic capillary chromatography with electrochemical detection
Determination of biogenic amines in cheese using HPLC technique and direct derivatization of acid extract
High performance liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry determination of biogenic amines in typical Piedmont cheeses
Determination of biogenic amines in fresh and processed meat by ion chromatography and integrated pulsed amperometric detection on Au electrode
Development of Immunoassays for Tyramine and Tryptamine Toxins of Phalaris aquatica L
Small organic molecular imprinted materials: their preparation and application
Integrating fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) sensor particles with a modular microfluidic platform for nanomolar smallmolecule detection directly in aqueous samples
Core-shell metal–organic frameworks/molecularly imprinted nanoparticles as absorbents for the detection of pyrraline in milk and milk powder
Large-scale fabrication of heavy doped carbon quantum dots with tunable-photoluminescence and sensitive fluorescence detection
Luminescent S-doped carbon dots: an emergent architecture for multimodal applications
Graphitized carbon dots emitting strong green photoluminescence
Graphene oxide-sensitized molecularly imprinted opto-polymers for charge-transfer fluorescent sensing of cyanoguanidine
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot
Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite
Amphiphilic egg-derived carbon dots: rapid plasma fabrication, pyrolysis process, and multicolor printing patterns
Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite
Bifunctional fluorescent carbon nanodots: green synthesis via soy milk and application as metal-free electrocatalysts for oxygen reduction
Carbon nanodots–chitosan composite film: A platform for protein immobilization, direct electrochemistry and bioelectrocatalysis
Bandgap-like strong fluorescence in functionalized carbon nanoparticles
Microwave-assisted rapid green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots from flour and their applications for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
One-pot synthesis of high fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their applications as probes for detection of tetracyclines
Photoluminescent carbon dots as biocompatible nanoprobes for targeting cancer cells in vitro
Carbon-dots derived from nanodiamond: photoluminescence tunable nanoparticles for cell imaging
Luminscent graphene quantum dots for organic photovoltaic devices
Carbon Quantum dots with photoenhanced hydrogen-bond catalytic activity in aldol condensations
ChemInform abstract: covalent organic frameworks (COFs): from design to applications
Molecular docking sites designed for the generation of highly crystalline covalent organic frameworks
Postsynthetically modified covalent organic frameworks for efficient and effective mercury removal
Grafting of quantum dots on covalent organic frameworks via areverse microemulsion for highly selective and sensitive protein optosensing
A novel quantum dots-labeled on the surface of molecularly imprinted polymer for turn-off optosensing of dicyandiamide in dairy products
